output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.5, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.6}}

Summary: The prose is readable, but the central claim is flatly contradicted by the numbers reported in the same paragraph. I flag the contradictions, then offer a revision that is honest about what the data show. I do not silently "fix" the numbers — only the author knows which figure is the typo.

## The blocking problem

The paper claims fine-tuning provides "direct causal evidence that improved self-recognition yields stronger self-preference" and a "positive, monotone relationship." The reported figures say the opposite:

- Pre-FT: recognition 54.2%, self-preference **62.1%**
- Post-FT: recognition 81.4%, self-preference **49.3%**

Recognition went up 27 points; self-preference went **down ~13 points**, landing at chance (50%). On its face, the headline experiment falsifies the hypothesis rather than confirming it.

This is then said to be consistent with a cross-family slope of β = 0.41 (positive). A 27-point recognition gain paired with a −13-point preference change implies a within-model slope near −0.48, which cannot be reconciled with β = +0.41 across families without further explanation.

Before any prose cleanup, one of the following must be true and must be made explicit:

1. The 49.3% is a typo (e.g., should be 79.3% or similar) — fix the number.
2. The hypothesis sign is wrong, and the actual finding is that improved recognition *reduces* self-preference toward chance — rewrite the framing, the interpretation, and the abstract. (This is the more interesting result, and it still supports the proposed mitigation: targeted suppression… no, wait — if better recognition *removes* self-preference, then the mitigation is targeted *enhancement* of recognition, not suppression. That conclusion also needs to flip.)
3. "Self-preference" is operationalized such that 50% is the *biased* baseline and deviations from 50% indicate something else — in which case the protocol description needs to make that clear, because Section 3 (as referenced) presumably treated 62.1% as the elevated condition.

## Other issues, conditional on the sign being resolved

- "Chance = 25%" for recognition implies a 4-way forced choice (self vs. three others), which is fine, but "comparable size" is doing a lot of work — Llama-3-70B against unnamed peers needs the peer list.
- The control conditions ("randomly-initialized recognition heads," "recognition-on-shuffled-labels") are introduced only in the last paragraph and never quantified. If they are load-bearing for the causal claim, they need their own numbers (recognition accuracy and self-preference rate under each control), not a one-line dismissal.
- "8,000 steps" with no batch size, learning rate, or held-in/held-out split definition is not reproducible. At minimum: dataset size, optimizer, and the held-in vs. held-out author partition.
- GPT-Neo-20B is 20B parameters; pairing it with Qwen-2-72B and Mistral-7B under "additional model families" is fine, but the β = 0.41 regression has n = 4 points (including Llama-3-70B). A slope with p < 0.01 from four points needs the underlying scatter shown, not just the p-value — and it needs to include the Llama-3-70B point, which (per the numbers above) appears to be an outlier in the wrong direction.
- "Most parsimonious account" overclaims from one intervention plus a 4-point regression. Soften to "consistent with."
- "Targeted suppression … offers a principled mitigation strategy" is a forward-looking claim that belongs in Discussion, not in the results section, and (per the sign issue) may point the wrong direction.

## Suggested revision (assuming interpretation #2 — that the finding is recognition training *eliminates* self-preference)

> We test whether self-recognition causally drives self-preference by intervening on the recognition signal and measuring the downstream change in preference. We fine-tune Llama-3-70B to distinguish its own generations from those of three comparably sized LLMs (Mistral-7B, Qwen-2-72B, GPT-Neo-20B; 4-way forced choice, chance = 25%) and re-evaluate self-preference using the pairwise protocol from Section 3.
>
> Before fine-tuning, the base model achieves 54.2% self-recognition accuracy and a 62.1% self-preference rate on held-out pairs (chance = 50%). After 8,000 steps of recognition-targeted fine-tuning on a held-in author distribution, recognition accuracy rises to 81.4% (+27.2 pts) while the self-preference rate falls to 49.3% — statistically indistinguishable from chance. Two controls (a randomly-initialized recognition head and recognition training on shuffled author labels) produce no comparable shift in either metric [Table X], ruling out generic capability gains.
>
> This pattern is the opposite of what a simple "better recognition → stronger preference" account predicts. Improving the model's ability to identify its own outputs eliminates, rather than amplifies, its tendency to prefer them. We replicate the direction across three additional base models [Figure Y]; the cross-model regression of Δ self-preference on Δ recognition accuracy yields β = −0.[??] (p < 0.01, n = 4). [If β really is +0.41, the within-Llama and across-family results disagree and that disagreement is the finding, not a footnote.]
>
> One reading is that self-preference in the base models reflects a *failure* of self-recognition — the model treats its own outputs as familiar-but-unattributed and rates them higher — and that sharpening attribution removes the halo. Under this reading, the mitigation implication reverses: strengthening, not suppressing, recognition signals could reduce self-preference in evaluator pipelines.

## Uncertainty

I cannot tell from the artifact alone whether the 49.3% is a typo or the actual finding; the suggested revision above commits to one reading. Do not adopt it without confirming which number is correct. The β sign in the cross-family regression similarly needs to be re-checked against the underlying per-model deltas — I have not seen those data.
